reasoning: I need to determine the relationship between position, initial_position, velocity, and time. Currently, position (10.0) equals initial_position (0.0) + velocity (5.0) * time (2.0). This suggests the equation might be position = initial_position + velocity * time. To verify this, I will change one variable at a time and observe the result. I'll start by increasing the initial_position to see if the position increases by the same amount.

I have observed that increasing initial_position from 0.0 to 10.0 caused the position to increase from 10.0 to 20.0, a change of +10.0. This suggests a linear relationship where position changes by the same amount as initial_position. The current data supports the hypothesis: position = initial_position + velocity * time. To further verify the velocity and time components, I will now increase the velocity.

I increased velocity from 5.0 to 10.0 (doubled it), and position increased from 20.0 to 30.0 (increased by 10.0). Since time is 2.0, the change in position (10.0) equals the change in velocity (5.0) multiplied by time (2.0). This confirms the velocity component of the equation. The data so far strongly supports position = initial_position + velocity * time. I will now test the time variable to fully confirm the relationship.

I have tested the effects of changing initial_position, velocity, and time on the position. Increasing initial_position by 10 increased position by 10. Doubling velocity increased position by (change in velocity * time). Increasing time increased position by (velocity * change in time). All observations are consistent with the linear equation position = initial_position + velocity * time. I have gathered sufficient evidence to conclude the relationship.
